summary: Gruber investigates how Sensor Tower, a marketing intelligence firm frequently cited by major news outlets for app usage statistics, actually collects its data. He examines each of Sensor Tower's consumer-facing apps — screen-time monitors, a game, web browser wrappers, and a VPN-based ad blocker — and finds that the data collection methods range from questionable to alarming. He argues that the user base of these apps (people who install third-party screen-time trackers instead of using built-in OS features, or who route all their traffic through a free VPN from a data analytics company) is unlikely to be representative of the general public. The piece concludes that the news media uncritically cites Sensor Tower's unverifiable estimates as fact without ever questioning the methodology or representativeness of the underlying data.
key_points: Sensor Tower collects usage data through its own consumer apps: screen-time monitors, browser extensions, wrapper browsers, and a VPN-based ad blocker — not through any privileged access to Apple, Google, or app developers' data.

Adblock Luna, Sensor Tower's VPN, routes all user internet traffic through their servers, giving them visibility into everything users do online — and they steer Android users toward sideloading rather than the Play Store version.

The user base of these apps is self-selected and likely unrepresentative: people seeking help with 'phone addiction,' technically naive users who install redundant screen-time tools, and people willing to trust a free VPN from a data analytics company.
Major news outlets like CNBC routinely cite Sensor Tower data about apps like Threads without disclosing or questioning the methodology behind those estimates.
There is no way to independently verify Sensor Tower's usage claims, unlike political polling which can be checked against election results.
money_quotes: Sensor Tower can only estimate these numbers, it can't know them.
Using a VPN for ad blocking, instead of a web browser extension, is like using a chainsaw to remove the kernels from a cob of corn — not just overkill but dangerous.
Quite literally, their users are their product.

The installation instructions for Adblock Luna are surely scary to non-technical laypeople, and they're downright terrifying to anyone expert enough to understand how VPNs work. So who is left? The ignorant but brazen.
This seems a bit like trying to glean beverage consumption statistics by polling self-professed alcoholics.

The news media so badly wants to know usage data that they just accept Sensor Tower's and other such firms' pronouncements at face value, without ever describing — let alone questioning — how they ostensibly know what they claim to know about very private data.
It's all unverifiable, but never reported as such.
key_insight: The tech usage statistics that major news outlets routinely cite as authoritative come from a company whose data is collected through dubious consumer apps with unrepresentative user bases, yet no one in the media ever questions the methodology.
